articles.title: Lloyds, Halifax and Bank of Scotland app users hit by outage
articles.summary: "We're aware some customers are having issues with our app and online banking. We're really sorry about this," Lloyds Bank posted on X.
articles.titleZh: 劳埃德银行、哈利法克斯银行和苏格兰银行的应用程序用户遭遇中断
articles.summaryZh: 劳埃德银行 (Lloyds Bank) 在 X 上发帖称：“我们知道一些客户在使用我们的应用程序和网上银行时遇到问题。我们对此感到非常抱歉。”

contentParagraphs.en: Site search25 minutes agoImran Rahman-JonesTechnology reporterEPA/ShutterstockLloyds Banking Group has apologised after thousands of customers experienced problems accessing online banking."We're aware some customers are having issues with our app and online banking. We're really sorry about this," Lloyds Bank posted on X. "We're working hard to fix it and will let you know as soon as we're back to normal," it added.Lloyds Banking Group says it is the UK's largest retail and commercial banking provider, with 26 million customers across Lloyds Bank, Halifax and Bank of Scotland.According to online outage tracker Downdetector, users started reporting problems at around 1115 BST. In response to a customer reporting they could not access their account, Halifax posted on X: "Some customers are having issues with accessing our Mobile App right now. Bear with us as we fix this."In a message on the Lloyds app, the bank said: "Sorry, we're having a few technical problems. Logging in again may fix the issue, but if this doesn't help, please try again later."It showed a 503 error message, which indicates a server is not ready to handle requests. In March, almost half a million Lloyds Banking Group customers saw other people's transactions or had their own data shared after an IT glitch.
contentParagraphs.zh: 网站搜索25分钟前Imran Rahman-Jones科技记者EPA/Shutterstock劳埃德银行集团在数千名客户在访问网上银行时遇到问题后道歉。劳埃德银行在X上发帖称：“我们知道一些客户在使用我们的应用程序和网上银行时遇到问题。我们对此感到非常抱歉。”它补充说：“我们正在努力解决这个问题，一旦恢复正常，我们会立即通知您。”是英国最大的零售和商业银行提供商，在劳埃德银行、哈利法克斯银行和苏格兰银行拥有 2600 万客户。根据在线中断跟踪器 DownDetector 的数据，用户在 BST 1115 左右开始报告问题。针对客户报告无法访问其帐户的情况，哈利法克斯在 X 上发帖称：“部分客户目前在访问我们的移动应用程序时遇到问题。请耐心等待我们解决此问题。”在劳埃德银行应用程序上的一条消息中，该银行表示：“抱歉，我们遇到了一些技术问题。再次登录可能会解决问题，但如果这没有帮助，请稍后再试。”它显示了 503 错误消息，这表明服务器尚未准备好处理请求。今年 3 月，近 50 万劳埃德银行集团 (Lloyds Banking Group) 客户在 IT 故障后看到了其他人的交易或共享了自己的数据。

articles.title: We finally know enough about how the brain breaks to focus on fixing it, experts say
articles.summary: The Allen Institute in Seattle says scientists have now learned enough about how the brain works to start fixing it when it breaks.
articles.titleZh: 专家表示，我们终于对大脑如何损坏有了足够的了解，可以专注于修复它
articles.summaryZh: 西雅图艾伦研究所表示，科学家们现在已经对大脑的工作原理有了足够的了解，可以在大脑崩溃时开始修复它。

contentParagraphs.en: Allen Institute scientist Aaron Garcia (right) and executive vice president Ed Lein (left) examine a section of human brain in the lab. The institute has launched the Brain Health accelerator, which will focus on using genetic therapy to develop treatments for a range of brain disorders.
contentParagraphs.zh: Allen Institute scientist Aaron Garcia (right) and executive vice president Ed Lein (left) examine a section of human brain in the lab. The institute has launched the Brain Health accelerator, which will focus on using genetic therapy to develop treatments for a range of brain disorders.

contentParagraphs.en: Erik Dinnel/Allen Institute
contentParagraphs.zh: Erik Dinnel/Allen Institute

contentParagraphs.en: Erik Dinnel/Allen Institute
contentParagraphs.zh: Erik Dinnel/Allen Institute

contentParagraphs.en: Scientists who've spent decades learning how the brain works say they're now ready to start fixing it when it breaks.
contentParagraphs.zh: Scientists who've spent decades learning how the brain works say they're now ready to start fixing it when it breaks.

contentParagraphs.en: That's the premise of the Brain Health accelerator, a collaborative effort launched by the Allen Institute in Seattle, which has become a major player in brain research.
contentParagraphs.zh: That's the premise of the Brain Health accelerator, a collaborative effort launched by the Allen Institute in Seattle, which has become a major player in brain research.

contentParagraphs.en: The initiative includes plans to develop new genetic therapies — a term that includes gene editing as well as traditional gene therapy — for diseases including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS, and Huntington's.
contentParagraphs.zh: The initiative includes plans to develop new genetic therapies — a term that includes gene editing as well as traditional gene therapy — for diseases including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS, and Huntington's.

contentParagraphs.en: "The latest genetic treatments allow scientists to control the activity of particular genes," says Ed Lein, who directs the institute's brain health programs. "That opens up the possibility for very specific precision therapies for brain disorders."
contentParagraphs.zh: "The latest genetic treatments allow scientists to control the activity of particular genes," says Ed Lein, who directs the institute's brain health programs. "That opens up the possibility for very specific precision therapies for brain disorders."

contentParagraphs.en: The accelerator is an outgrowth of the BRAIN Initiative, an ambitious research program unveiled by President Obama in 2013. The goal of this public-private partnership was to create tools that would allow scientists to see the brain's inner workings, and, eventually, to develop treatments.
contentParagraphs.zh: The accelerator is an outgrowth of the BRAIN Initiative, an ambitious research program unveiled by President Obama in 2013. The goal of this public-private partnership was to create tools that would allow scientists to see the brain's inner workings, and, eventually, to develop treatments.

contentParagraphs.en: But the effort has progressed far faster than many scientists expected.
contentParagraphs.zh: But the effort has progressed far faster than many scientists expected.

contentParagraphs.en: "I am shocked at how far we've come in the last 10, 12 years," says John Ngai, a senior investigator at the National Institutes of Health who directs the BRAIN Initiative. "It's just been beyond my wildest imagination — and I've been accused of having a pretty good imagination."
contentParagraphs.zh: "I am shocked at how far we've come in the last 10, 12 years," says John Ngai, a senior investigator at the National Institutes of Health who directs the BRAIN Initiative. "It's just been beyond my wildest imagination — and I've been accused of having a pretty good imagination."

contentParagraphs.en: From mice to humans
contentParagraphs.zh: From mice to humans

contentParagraphs.en: The Allen Institute's Brain Health accelerator is attracting a range of scientists intent on translating their knowledge about the brain into treatments for its disorders.
contentParagraphs.zh: The Allen Institute's Brain Health accelerator is attracting a range of scientists intent on translating their knowledge about the brain into treatments for its disorders.

contentParagraphs.en: Jeff Carroll, for example, was a teenager when he learned that his mother had Huntington's Disease, a fatal, inherited disorder that destroys brain cells.
contentParagraphs.zh: Jeff Carroll, for example, was a teenager when he learned that his mother had Huntington's Disease, a fatal, inherited disorder that destroys brain cells.

contentParagraphs.en: "The whole reason I'm in science started with this frustration with not being able to understand what was happening with my mom," he says.
contentParagraphs.zh: "The whole reason I'm in science started with this frustration with not being able to understand what was happening with my mom," he says.

contentParagraphs.en: Carroll eventually learned that he, too, carried the Huntington's gene.
contentParagraphs.zh: Carroll eventually learned that he, too, carried the Huntington's gene.

contentParagraphs.en: He spent years at the University of Washington studying mice with the condition, which causes nerve cells to produce toxic levels of a certain protein.
contentParagraphs.zh: He spent years at the University of Washington studying mice with the condition, which causes nerve cells to produce toxic levels of a certain protein.

contentParagraphs.en: To Carroll, the solution seemed obvious: "Since we know that all the bad stuff in Huntington's comes from this one gene … let's get rid of that gene."
contentParagraphs.zh: To Carroll, the solution seemed obvious: "Since we know that all the bad stuff in Huntington's comes from this one gene … let's get rid of that gene."

contentParagraphs.en: But finding a way to do that was beyond the reach of his own relatively small lab at a university. So Carroll joined the accelerator effort.
contentParagraphs.zh: But finding a way to do that was beyond the reach of his own relatively small lab at a university. So Carroll joined the accelerator effort.

contentParagraphs.en: "It's difficult to do the scale of research that you need with a team of five or six or even 10 people," he says. "The hundreds of people they have here at the Allen Institute [allow for] an entirely different approach to science."
contentParagraphs.zh: "It's difficult to do the scale of research that you need with a team of five or six or even 10 people," he says. "The hundreds of people they have here at the Allen Institute [allow for] an entirely different approach to science."

contentParagraphs.en: And one of the first targets for that approach will be Huntington's disease.
contentParagraphs.zh: And one of the first targets for that approach will be Huntington's disease.

contentParagraphs.en: Carroll says he's optimistic.
contentParagraphs.zh: Carroll says he's optimistic.

contentParagraphs.en: He notes that genetic therapies have already had success treating at least one nerve disorder, a rare genetic condition called spinal muscular atrophy. It's caused by a gene mutation that leads to the death of motor neurons in the spinal cord.
contentParagraphs.zh: He notes that genetic therapies have already had success treating at least one nerve disorder, a rare genetic condition called spinal muscular atrophy. It's caused by a gene mutation that leads to the death of motor neurons in the spinal cord.

contentParagraphs.en: "Every kid with this horrible mutation died when they were like 18 months [old]," Carroll says, "and now they're going to high school. So things that were unimaginable can change."
contentParagraphs.zh: "Every kid with this horrible mutation died when they were like 18 months [old]," Carroll says, "and now they're going to high school. So things that were unimaginable can change."

contentParagraphs.en: Carroll says his goal is to help accelerate that change.
contentParagraphs.zh: Carroll says his goal is to help accelerate that change.

contentParagraphs.en: Big science, shared
contentParagraphs.zh: Big science, shared

contentParagraphs.en: The Allen Institute was founded in 2003 by the late Paul Allen of Microsoft fame, and his younger sister, Jody Allen.
contentParagraphs.zh: The Allen Institute was founded in 2003 by the late Paul Allen of Microsoft fame, and his younger sister, Jody Allen.

contentParagraphs.en: Its goal was to speed up science research. And it has done so by developing technologies that allow scientists to rapidly characterize and map millions of cells.
contentParagraphs.zh: Its goal was to speed up science research. And it has done so by developing technologies that allow scientists to rapidly characterize and map millions of cells.

contentParagraphs.en: "We now have a complete description of the types of cells that make up the brain, and also the genetic underpinnings of their properties," Lein says. "This foundation then lets you study disease."
contentParagraphs.zh: "We now have a complete description of the types of cells that make up the brain, and also the genetic underpinnings of their properties," Lein says. "This foundation then lets you study disease."

contentParagraphs.en: Allen scientists have already begun studying how Alzheimer's disease changes nerve cells in the brain.
contentParagraphs.zh: Allen scientists have already begun studying how Alzheimer's disease changes nerve cells in the brain.

contentParagraphs.en: "It affects very specific types of neurons that are lost early in the disease and then over the course of the disease," Lein says.
contentParagraphs.zh: "It affects very specific types of neurons that are lost early in the disease and then over the course of the disease," Lein says.

contentParagraphs.en: Genetic therapies designed to protect these neurons might delay or prevent symptoms, he says. Similar approaches might also work to preserve the neurons affected by Parkinson's or ALS.
contentParagraphs.zh: Genetic therapies designed to protect these neurons might delay or prevent symptoms, he says. Similar approaches might also work to preserve the neurons affected by Parkinson's or ALS.

contentParagraphs.en: Ultimately, though, the Allen Institute may not be the place that develops these therapies. Its policy of making its databases available to anyone means that scientists around the globe can help look for answers.

articles.title: Ferrari sign Leclerc on new long-term contract before home F1 Monaco GP
articles.summary: Charles Leclerc has extended his contract with Ferrari for an undisclosed length, having joined the F1 team in 2019.
articles.titleZh: 法拉利在主场 F1 摩纳哥大奖赛前与勒克莱尔签订新的长期合同
articles.summaryZh: 查尔斯·勒克莱尔 (Charles Leclerc) 于 2019 年加入 F1 车队，与法拉利续约，合同期限未公开。

contentParagraphs.en: Charles Leclerc has extended his contract with Ferrari for an undisclosed length, having joined the F1 team in 2019.Charles Leclerc will continue to race for Ferrari after ⁠the Italian Formula One team announced a long-term contract extension before his home Monaco Grand Prix.The 28-year-old Monegasque has driven for the Maranello-based outfit in F1 since ⁠2019, winning eight races, and his last contract extension came in 2024.Recommended Stories list of 4 itemslist 1 of 4World Cup contenders: Are Morocco ready to go all the way this time?list 2 of 4Cape Verde football team arrives in the US for the World Cuplist 3 of 4US artist sues FIFA over destruction of Dallas whale mural for World Cuplist 4 of 4‘We aren’t the big favourites,’ Brazil’s Casemiro saysend of list“I couldn’t be happier to continue this journey with Scuderia Ferrari HP. It has always been so much more than just a team to me,” Leclerc said in a statement issued by Ferrari on Wednesday.Leclerc is currently third in the 2026 drivers’ championship but 56 points behind Mercedes’ Kimi Antonelli, who is 43 ahead of teammate and closest rival George Russell after winning the last four races.He has raced more times for Ferrari (155) than any other driver in the team’s history, other than now-retired seven-time world champion Michael Schumacher, and also ranks ‌second for pole positions behind the German.Ferrari did not specify the duration of his new contract, saying only that Leclerc would continue for the “coming seasons” in the championship.“Charles has been part of the Ferrari family for many years now and this renewal feels like something very natural for us,” team principal Fred Vasseur said.“Over these seasons, we have seen him grow to become not only one of the strongest drivers in Formula One, but also a person who is completely at one with ⁠the team and everything Ferrari represents.“We know how much this project means to ⁠him and we are happy to continue working towards our shared goals.”Ferrari’s Charles Leclerc finished second at the Canadian Grand Prix in March [Mathieu Belanger/Reuters]Leclerc joined the Ferrari Academy in 2016, won the Formula Two title in 2017 and made his F1 debut with Ferrari-powered Sauber (now Audi) in 2018.Leclerc’s teammate is the UK’s seven-time world champion Lewis Hamilton, who ⁠joined from Mercedes last year on a multi-year contract and is expected to continue next season, when he will be 41 years old.Hamilton is fourth in the ⁠standings, three points behind Leclerc.Ferrari have not taken a Grand Prix ⁠victory since 2024, or a championship since 2008, but Hamilton won a Saturday sprint race in China last season and finished second in Canada last month.Leclerc has had two podium finishes this season – third in Australia and Japan – and will be heavily fancied for his ‌home race this weekend, with Ferrari expected to be competitive at the slowest circuit on the calendar, where top speed is less of a factor and grid position is crucial.Growing up in the principality, Leclerc in 2024 ‌became ‌the first local to win the Monaco Grand Prix in the championship era and has taken pole there three times in the last five years.
contentParagraphs.zh: 查尔斯·勒克莱尔 (Charles Leclerc) 于 2019 年加入 F1 车队，与法拉利续签了合同，具体期限未公开。在意大利一级方程式车队在家乡摩纳哥大奖赛之前宣布长期续约后，查尔斯·勒克莱尔 (Charles Leclerc) 将继续为法拉利比赛。这位 28 岁的摩纳哥车手自 2019 年以来一直为这家总部位于马拉内罗的 F1 车队效力，赢得了 8 场比赛，他最后一次续约是在 2019 年。 2024.推荐故事 4 项列表 4 项世界杯竞争者中的 1 项：摩洛哥这次准备好一路走来了吗？ 列表 2 项（共 4 项） 佛得角足球队抵达美国参加世界杯 列表项 3 项 美国艺术家因破坏世界杯达拉斯鲸鱼壁画而起诉国际足联 4 项中的“我们不是大热门，”巴西队的卡塞米罗说 列表结束“我很高兴继续勒克莱尔在周三法拉利发布的一份声明中表示，“与法拉利车队的这段旅程对我来说不仅仅是一支球队。”勒克莱尔目前在 2026 年车手积分榜上排名第三，但落后梅赛德斯的基米·安东内利 56 分，后者在赢得过去四场比赛后领先队友和最接近的对手乔治·拉塞尔 43 分。他为法拉利参赛的次数（155 次）比车队历史上任何其他车手都多。现已退役的七届世界冠军迈克尔·舒马赫，杆位排名也仅次于德国人。法拉利没有具体说明他的新合同期限，只表示勒克莱尔将在“未来几个赛季”继续参加锦标赛。“查尔斯已经成为法拉利家族的一员很多年了，这次续约对我们来说是非常自然的事情，”车队负责人弗雷德·瓦瑟尔说。“在这些赛季中，我们看到他不仅成长为一级方程式中最强大的车手之一，法拉利车队的查尔斯·勒克莱尔 (Charles Leclerc) 在 3 月份的加拿大大奖赛上获得第二名 [Mathieu Belanger/Reuters] 勒克莱尔于 2016 年加入法拉利学院，于 2017 年赢得二级方程式冠军，并于 2017 年随法拉利动力的索伯车队（现为奥迪）首次亮相 F1。 2018年。勒克莱尔的队友是英国七届世界冠军刘易斯·汉密尔顿，他去年以多年合同从梅赛德斯加盟，预计下个赛季将继续效力，届时他将年满41岁。汉密尔顿在积分榜上排名第四，落后勒克莱尔三分。法拉利自2024年以来没有获得过大奖赛胜利，自2008年以来也没有获得过冠军，但汉密尔顿赢得了周六的冲刺赛上赛季在中国的比赛中，上个月在加拿大获得第二名。勒克莱尔本赛季两次登上领奖台，在澳大利亚和日本获得第三名，并且本周末的主场比赛将受到广泛关注，法拉利预计将在日历上最慢的赛道上具有竞争力，最高时速不那么重要，发车位置至关重要。勒克莱尔在公国长大，于 2024 年成为冠军时代第一个赢得摩纳哥大奖赛的当地人，并且过去五年里，他曾三次获得杆位。

articles.title: Meta workers can opt out of being tracked at work - but only for half an hour at a time
articles.summary: According to an internal memo, new controls will allow employees to pause the data collection for "up to 30 minutes at a time".
articles.titleZh: 元工作人员可以选择在工作中不被跟踪，但每次只能半小时
articles.summaryZh: 根据一份内部备忘录，新的控制措施将允许员工“一次最多暂停 30 分钟”的数据收集。

contentParagraphs.en: Meta workers can opt out of being tracked at work - but only for half an hour at a time2 hours agoLaura Cress,technology reporterandOsmond Chia,business reporterGetty ImagesMeta is scaling back its plan to start tracking its employees' computer activity, according to an internal memo sent on Tuesday.In April the company received criticism from its own staff after it announced a new tool would log their keystrokes and mouse clicks to train its AI models.Now, according to Reuters, new controls will allow employees to pause the data collection for "up to 30 minutes at a time" as well as request exemptions from the initiative altogether.Meta declined to comment on the record.It follows weeks of backlash from employees, including some who started a petition against the move which now has more than 1,500 signatures. During the initial announcement of the tool, called the Model Capability Initiative (MCI), Meta told the BBC: "If we're building agents to help people complete everyday tasks using computers, our models need real examples of how people actually use them."It added that the data was "not used for any other purpose," and the tool had "safeguards in place to protect sensitive content".But workers were not impressed, with one Meta employee, who asked not to be identified, telling the BBC that having their actions train AI models felt "very dystopian" - as workers expected a slew of additional job cuts.Another person who recently left the company told the BBC the tracking tool was "just the latest way they're shoving AI down everyone's throat".An internal memo - seen by Reuters - was reportedly authored by Stephane Kasriel, a vice president in Meta's Superintelligence Labs unit.In it, he said the team behind the MCI had introduced "several optimizations" to reduce its impact on laptop battery life.This change came after reports that employees were finding the tool consumed so much data it was causing their internet usage to surge when working from home."While we remain confident in the privacy protections we put in place at launch, which went through several layers of risk review, we have heard your concerns about personal data on work devices, battery life, and wanting more control over when capturing happens," Kasriel said in the memo.
contentParagraphs.zh: 根据周二发送的一份内部备忘录，Meta 员工可以选择在工作中不被跟踪，但每次只能半小时 2 小时前，技术记者劳拉·克雷斯 (Laura Cress) 和商业记者奥斯蒙德·谢 (Osmond Chia) 盖蒂图片社 (Getty Images) 根据周二发送的一份内部备忘录，Meta 正在缩减开始跟踪员工计算机活动的计划。4 月份，该公司宣布一款新工具将记录他们的击键和鼠标点击来训练其人工智能模型，之后受到了员工的批评。现在，据路透社报道，新的控制措施将允许员工暂停数据收集“一次最多 30 分钟”，并请求完全豁免该举措。Meta 拒绝公开发表评论。在此之前，员工们已经持续数周强烈反对，其中一些人发起了反对这一举措的请愿书，目前已有 1,500 多个签名。在这个名为“模型能力计划”(MCI) 的工具最初发布期间，Meta 告诉 BBC：“如果我们要构建代理来帮助人们使用计算机完成日常任务，我们的模型需要人们实际如何使用它们的真实示例。”它补充说，这些数据“没有用于任何其他目的”，并且该工具“有适当的保护措施来保护敏感内容”。但工人们对此并不以为然，一位不愿透露姓名的 Meta 员工告诉 BBC，让他们的行为训练人工智能模型感觉“非常糟糕”。另一位最近离开公司的人士告诉 BBC，追踪工具“只是他们将人工智能强加给每个人的最新方式”。路透社看到的一份内部备忘录据报道是由 Meta 超级智能实验室部门副总裁 Stephane Kasriel 撰写的。在备忘录中，他表示 MCI 背后的团队引入了“几项优化”，以减少对笔记本电脑电池的影响这一变化是在有报道称员工发现该工具消耗了太多数据，导致他们在家工作时互联网使用量激增的情况下做出的。“虽然我们对发布时实施的隐私保护仍然充满信心，该保护经过了多层风险审查，但我们听到了你们对工作设备上的个人数据、电池寿命以及希望在捕获发生时进行更多控制的担忧，”卡斯瑞尔在备忘录中表示。

articles.title: The Trump administration aims to pull science funding under tighter political control
articles.summary: A Trump administration rule could shift how science funding works in the U.S. The administration says its an effort to deter waste, but science advocates say it will compromise scientific integrity.
articles.titleZh: 特朗普政府旨在将科学资助置于更严格的政治控制之下
articles.summaryZh: 特朗普政府的一项规定可能会改变美国科学资助的运作方式。政府表示，这是为了阻止浪费，但科学倡导者表示，这将损害科学诚信。

contentParagraphs.en: A Trump administration rule could shift how science funding works in the U.S. The administration says its an effort to deter waste, but science advocates say it will compromise scientific integrity.
contentParagraphs.zh: A Trump administration rule could shift how science funding works in the U.S. The administration says its an effort to deter waste, but science advocates say it will compromise scientific integrity.

contentParagraphs.en: AILSA CHANG, HOST: President Trump continues to whittle away at federal science funding. And now his administration is attempting to put the decisions about what gets funding under tighter political control. The move is upsetting many researchers and activists like Cole Donovan from the group Stand Up for Science.COLE DONOVAN: This would be the end of American science as we know it, and we are going to make sure that it doesn't fade quietly into the night.CHANG: NPR's Katia Riddle has been reporting on the rule change behind this latest development and joins us now. Hi, Katia.KATIA RIDDLE, BYLINE: Hi, Ailsa.CHANG: OK, so tell us more about what changed exactly in these rules, like why it's such a big deal here.RIDDLE: Well, you know, it's a bureaucratic rule change. And it sounds small, but it really carries a significant potential to disrupt the way we fund science in the United States.CHANG: OK. Yeah.RIDDLE: Quick review of how science funding works - Congress approves money for agencies like the National Institutes of Health, the National Science Foundation, and then those agencies are empowered to make grants to scientists to do things like cancer research or, you know, make earthquake early warning systems. Last week, the administration proposed a new rule that's now under review. It makes a lot of different changes, but one big one is it would give political appointees and the White House a much larger role over what kind of science gets funded. Scientists and advocates are very upset about this. The Infectious Diseases Society of America, for example, issued a statement yesterday that was titled, quote, "Proposed Rule Would Replace Scientific Merit With McCarthy Era Politics," unquote.CHANG: Wow.RIDDLE: That's a reference, of course, to a period in the 1950s when government officials scrutinized people's ideology and politics rather than their credentials.CHANG: Oof. OK, so based on that, how do scientists think this rule change might play out?RIDDLE: Well, first of all, it literally could affect any kind of federally funded science in the United States - public health, vaccines, biotechnology, social and behavioral science, climate. And one thing that's really animating people - under this new rule, senior political appointees, not just scientists, would review grants in order to assess whether they align with the president's priorities before awards are made. I talked to Elizabeth Ginexi. She's a former federal scientist, and she has a lot of objections to empowering Russell Vought - he's the head of the White House's Office of Management and Budget - with this kind of authority.ELIZABETH GINEXI: When we're designing a study to a new cancer therapeutic, do you want Russell Vought, who is not a scientist, to determine which immunotherapy is ready to go into a phase 3 trial?RIDDLE: A phase 3 trial, by the way, is the kind that determines if a drug is safe and effective in humans.CHANG: Right - very, very important - OK, so then what is the rationale behind these changes, at least from the Trump administration's point of view?RIDDLE: Right. So the White House and OMB officials declined a request for interview for this story, but they sent a statement over that said this rule change will, quote, "improve the ability of agencies to identify and respond to waste, fraud and abuse and align with agency priorities." Critics say there's no evidence that the existing peer-reviewed system needs such sweeping changes.CHANG: I mean, this rule change is just a proposal right now, right? Like, how soon could it be a reality?RIDDLE: Yeah, we're in an open period comment, where the public has 45 days to weigh in. Congress could, in theory, block this rule, and no one I spoke with thinks that is likely. Like I said, it's raised a lot of resistance from folks in the science community, and they plan to continue organizing amongst themselves and the public to push back.CHANG: That is NPR's Katia Riddle. Thank you, Katia.RIDDLE: Thank you.

articles.title: Israel is building more military posts in Gaza, satellite imagery shows
articles.summary: An investigation by Al Jazeera has identified 40 distinct Israeli military outposts entrenched within Gaza.
articles.titleZh: 卫星图像显示，以色列正在加沙建立更多军事哨所
articles.summaryZh: 半岛电视台的一项调查发现，加沙地带有 40 个不同的以色列军事前哨基地。

contentParagraphs.en: Israel was supposed to fully withdraw its troops from Gaza as part of the ceasefire signed in October. Instead of pulling back, Israeli forces are quietly cementing permanent, heavily fortified military posts across the besieged enclave, according to satellite imagery analysed by Al Jazeera.An investigation by Al Jazeera’s Open Source Unit, analysing satellite data up to May 2026, has identified 40 distinct Israeli military outposts entrenched within Gaza. Crucially, the analysis proves that eight of these bases were constructed entirely from scratch after the October 2025 truce went into effect, with one site still undergoing active construction.(Al Jazeera)This physical entrenchment mirrors the increasingly overt territorial ambitions of Israel’s leadership. Speaking at a recent conference, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu confirmed directives to permanently seize the vast majority of the Strip.Israeli forces have pulled back to the “Yellow Line”, which refers to the buffer and military zones comprising some 60 percent of the enclave’s territory.“We are currently squeezing Hamas; we now control 60 percent of the territory,” Netanyahu stated, before addressing a crowd member who shouted for complete annexation: “Let’s go step by step. First of all, 70. Let’s start with that.”Desecration and new constructionsThe satellite analysis exposes a systematic effort to build a sustainable, long-term military infrastructure rather than temporary observation posts.The newly established installations are strategically dispersed: Two in northern Gaza, two in the central region, one east of the Netzarim Corridor, and three in the southern city of Khan Younis.In one of the most glaring examples of this spatial takeover, Israeli forces established a new military base directly atop the ruins of the Eastern Cemetery in Khan Younis.Satellite images show that engineering works on the bulldozed burial ground began in November 2025. By May 18, 2026, the site was fully equipped with vehicle staging areas and repetitive structures, likely used for troop housing and operational meetings.A similar pattern of rapid militarisation is visible in northern Gaza. In Beit Lahiya, an area that appeared completely clear in October 2025 photos, satellite imagery captured the sudden onset of engineering works by mid-November.By May 2026, a fully enclosed military structure with internal facilities had materialised.Fortifying the front linesBeyond the construction of new bases, the Israeli military is aggressively upgrading its preexisting positions within the “Yellow Line” – the temporary demarcation zones where forces were permitted to remain pending a full withdrawal.East of Gaza City, a military outpost expanded its surface area by an estimated 70 percent between October 2025 and May 2026.The upgraded site now features extensive internal restructuring, new staging zones for armoured vehicles, and bolstered fortifications. In central Gaza, satellite sensors detected deep defensive trenches being excavated around an existing military installation, signalling a deliberate shift towards long-term field endurance.The strategic intent of this infrastructure is most evident around the Netzarim Corridor, a route used by the Israeli army to physically sever northern Gaza from the south.The Open Source Unit identified three separate military outposts guarding the areas east of and immediately around this axis, ensuring Israel’s continued chokehold over movement between the two halves of the enclave.Just east of this corridor in Juhor ad-Dik, a patch of open land was rapidly transformed into an emerging military base after earthworks began in March 2026.Encircling a battered populationThe geographical distribution of these 40 military outposts reveals a deliberate strategy of encirclement. The bases, connected by a network of earthen berms, trenches, and internal military roads, tightly surround Palestinian population centres from multiple directions.This suffocating architecture severely restricts the ability of civilians to move freely or access their lands, particularly in areas abutting the Israeli deployment lines.The expanding occupation stands in direct violation of the Untied States-brokered October 2025 ceasefire agreement, which was based on a 21-point peace plan proposed by President Donald Trump. The framework demanded an end to the hostilities, the immediate entry of aid, the disarmament of Hamas, and a phased Israeli withdrawal.Instead, the Palestinian death toll continues to rise. Since the war began in October 2023, Israeli attacks have killed nearly 73,000 Palestinians and injured more than 172,919, with women and children making up most of the casualties.According to Gaza’s Ministry of Health data, the violence did not end with the truce; at least 929 Palestinians have been killed and 2,811 injured in the seven months since the ceasefire officially took effect.
contentParagraphs.zh: 作为十月份签署的停火协议的一部分，以色列本应从加沙全面撤军。根据半岛电视台分析的卫星图像，以色列军队没有撤退，而是悄悄地在被围困的飞地上巩固了永久性的、戒备森严的军事哨所。半岛电视台开源部门的一项调查分析了截至 2026 年 5 月的卫星数据，发现了盘踞在加沙的 40 个不同的以色列军事前哨基地。至关重要的是，分析证明，其中 8 个基地是在 2025 年 10 月停火协议生效后完全从头开始建造的，其中一个基地仍在积极建设中。（半岛电视台）这种物理防御工事反映了以色列领导层日益公开的领土野心。在最近的一次会议上，以色列总理本杰明·内塔尼亚胡确认了永久占领加沙地带绝大多数地区的指示。以色列军队已撤回“黄线”，即占飞地约 60% 领土的缓冲区和军事区。内塔尼亚胡表示：“我们目前正在挤压哈马斯；我们现在控制着 60% 的领土。”首先，70。让我们从这个开始。“亵渎和新建筑卫星分析揭示了建设可持续、长期军事基础设施而不是临时观察所的系统性努力。新建立的设施在战略上是分散的：两个在加沙北部，两个在中部地区，一个在内扎里姆走廊以东，三个在南部城市汗尤尼斯。在这种空间接管的最明显的例子之一中，以色列军队直接在加沙地带的顶部建立了一个新的军事基地。汗尤尼斯东部公墓遗址。卫星图像显示，推土机墓地的工程工作于 2025 年 11 月开始。到 2026 年 5 月 18 日，该场地已完全配备了车辆停靠区和重复结构，可能用于部队住房和作战会议。在加沙北部也可以看到类似的快速军事化模式。在 2025 年 10 月的照片中看起来完全清晰的拜特拉希亚地区，卫星图像捕捉到了 11 月中旬突然开始的工程施工。到 2026 年 5 月，一座具有内部设施的全封闭军事建筑已经建成。加强前线除了建设新基地之外，以色列军方正在积极升级其在“黄线”内的现有阵地，即允许部队在全面撤军之前停留的临时分界区。加沙城军事前哨基地的面积在 2025 年 10 月至 2026 年 5 月期间估计扩大了 70%。升级后的基地现在拥有广泛的内部重组、新的装甲车集结区和加固的防御工事。在加沙中部，卫星传感器检测到现有军事设施周围正在挖掘深防御壕沟，这表明以色列有意向长期野外持久性转变。该基础设施的战略意图在 Netzarim 走廊周围最为明显，以色列军队使用这条路线将加沙北部与南部进行物理隔离。开源部门确定了三个独立的军事前哨，守卫着该轴线以东和紧邻该轴线的地区，确保以色列继续扼制加沙地带两半之间的运动。就在Juhor ad-Dik这条走廊的东边，2026年3月土方工程开始后，一块开阔的土地迅速转变为新兴的军事基地。包围饱受摧残的人口这40个军事前哨的地理分布揭示了一种刻意的包围战略。这些基地由土堤、战壕和内部军事道路网络连接起来，从多个方向紧紧包围巴勒斯坦人口中心。这种令人窒息的建筑严重限制了平民自由行动或进入其土地的能力，特别是在毗邻以色列部署线的地区。不断扩大的占领直接违反了美国斡旋的 2025 年 10 月停火协议，该协议基于唐纳德·特朗普总统提出的 21 点和平计划。该框架要求停止敌对行动、立即提供援助、解除哈马斯武装以及分阶段以色列撤军。相反，巴勒斯坦死亡人数继续上升。自2023年10月战争爆发以来，以色列的袭击已造成近73,000名巴勒斯坦人死亡，超过172,919人受伤，其中妇女和儿童占伤亡人数的大部分。加沙卫生部的数据显示，暴力并没有随着停战而结束；冲突并没有结束。自停火正式生效以来的七个月内，至少有 929 名巴勒斯坦人被杀，2,811 人受伤。

articles.title: As the tech mega-IPO race heats up, has OpenAI missed its moment?
articles.summary:
With rivals racing to market to raise ‘eye-popping sums’, the spotlight is now on the AI sector’s one-time ‘poster child’
A year is a long time in AI. Just 12 months ago, Sam Altman was predicting his company OpenAI would build a super intelligence and fundamentally remake societ
articles.titleZh: 随着科技巨头 IPO 竞赛的白热化，OpenAI 错过了时机吗？
articles.summaryZh:
随着竞争对手竞相进入市场筹集“令人瞠目结舌的资金”，现在的焦点集中在人工智能领域一度的“典范”上
对于人工智能来说，一年是一段很长的时间。就在 12 个月前，Sam Altman 还预测他的公司 OpenAI 将打造一个超级智能并从根本上重塑社会

contentParagraphs.en: A year is a long time in AI. Just 12 months ago, Sam Altman was predicting his company OpenAI would build a super intelligence and fundamentally remake society. Now the boss of the ChatGPT developer is walking back those ideas after failing to make money from ads and erotic chatbots.Meanwhile, rivals are storming ahead with plans to expand and go public on the stock market, in what is widely expected to be a season of record-setting initial public offerings (IPOs).Elon Musk’s SpaceX, which owns xAI, is going to float this month, while Anthropic confidentially filed for an IPO on Monday in what the New York Times said could be a “once in a generation” moment for Wall Street. And the AI chip designer and Google’s parent company, Alphabet, is raising $80bn (£60bn) to fund a larger AI infrastructure buildout – in what analysts say is the largest equity fundraising ever.These flotations – and OpenAI’s, if it happens – will reveal much about the ambitions and state of the AI economy. The numbers involved are eye-popping; already there is talk of whether the IPOs themselves might strain limited pools of capital. Questions hang over the progress of the AI infrastructure build-out, and the degree to which AI tools can replace human labour. Some of the biggest questions hang over OpenAI, the “poster child” of the boom.One might have been forgiven, back in the halcyon days of early 2025, for thinking that money – or at least, the question of a profit – wasn’t everything to Altman.The OpenAI chief executive was blogging about creating “science fiction” tools that could “massively accelerate scientific discovery” and “massively increase abundance”. The US vice-president, JD Vance, was reportedly among those reading with concern the doomsday scenario AI 2027, which envisions a company loosely modelled on OpenAI building a super intelligence that destroys humanity.Vast sums chased these visions of ultimate power; OpenAI announced a $500bn investment into US AI infrastructure, called Stargate, “to secure American leadership in AI”. (For good measure, it announced a less-expensive Stargate in the UK, which it shelved in April this year.)But in 2026, it turns out that money is important – and OpenAI needs more. It may not be the best time to float, but there might not be a better one.The bare facts of the business have not changed in the past year, even if belief in the machine god has tempered. This winter, OpenAI successively announced – then failed to execute – several strategies to monetise ChatGPT, including advertisements, which Altman had earlier said was a “uniquely unsettling” proposition and a “last resort”. Meanwhile, after teasing the idea of erotic chatbots in late 2025, OpenAI backed out of the idea in March, saying its other work was a “higher priority”.The Information reports that OpenAI made revenue of $5.7bn in the first quarter of this year – but with adjusted negative margins of -122%, meaning it lost $1.22 for every dollar it spent. These numbers are impossible to confirm, as OpenAI does not release financial details, but underscore a fundamental truth of the AI economy: the computing power that makes ChatGPT possible is expensive, and isn’t getting cheaper with scale.Can OpenAI – valued at $852bn at its last funding round – mount a successful IPO? What happens if it all goes sideways?Russ Mould, an investment director at AJ Bell, says: “Until we see the numbers, it’s very difficult for us to assess the valuation. We need to know what the projected market cap might be, what the revenues are … what cashflow looks like.”In some ways, OpenAI is acting like a mature, IPO-ready business, says Adrian Cox, a thematic strategist at the Deutsche Bank Research Institute. At an event earlier this month in Australia, Altman struck a sharply different note around AI-related job loss than he did a year ago, saying he didn’t expect a “jobs apocalypse.” (Last year, he said that people will “find new things to do, new ways to be useful to each other” following AI-related social change.)“The demands of a public company are very different from a private company and the transparency that is required in going public has caused not only a change in strategy, but also a change in tone around the company,” says Cox.“The company’s clearly engaging in an important shift as it positions itself as the kind of mature company that you would expect to go public.”Clashes have been reported between Sarah Friar, OpenAI’s chief financial officer, and Altman, over the company’s timeline to go public. Photograph: Bloomberg/GettyThere are caveats, though, including the reported clashes between OpenAI’s chief financial officer, Sarah Friar, and Altman over the company’s timeline to go public. Friar has reportedly expressed doubts that OpenAI is ready to go public this year, and reservations about whether it can cover its computing costs.The question is, can it afford not to? “This feels like the year for mega AI IPOs,” says Cox. “The demand seems to be there from retail investors. The demand is also there from users for AI.”There could be consequences if it does not. The market is at record highs, having weathered Iran-related wobbles earlier this year. There is only so much capital available to share with others planning to go public such as Anthropic – which last week overtook OpenAI with a valuation of $965bn – and SpaceX.Cox says: “Some might say that there is only a finite amount of financing to go around, there may be a downside to waiting under those circumstances.”Mould says: “You’ve got a market with enormous potential with a lot of very, very big people jockeying … very big players jockeying for position. We don’t know who’s going to win. They can’t all be valued as winners in the long run.”What happens if OpenAI’s stock is a flop? For some – including Gary Marcus, a noted AI critic and professor at New York University – it could signal the start of something bigger: cracks in the “fantastical thinking” that unde

contentParagraphs.en: Drinking water is one of the main ways people are exposed to per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or "PFAS." These chemicals have been linked to negative health outcomes, including certain cancers.

contentParagraphs.en: "Forever chemicals" are everywhere — in our drinking water, in our food and in products like nonstick frying pans, raincoats and even some types of floss.
contentParagraphs.zh: "Forever chemicals" are everywhere — in our drinking water, in our food and in products like nonstick frying pans, raincoats and even some types of floss.

contentParagraphs.en: Also known as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or "PFAS," these chemicals have been linked to negative health outcomes, including certain cancers.
contentParagraphs.zh: Also known as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or "PFAS," these chemicals have been linked to negative health outcomes, including certain cancers.

contentParagraphs.en: PFAS are strong and can repel both water and oil, which is why they're widely used in manufacturing. But they don't break down easily in the environment, and they can linger in our bodies. In fact, according to the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, nearly everyone in the United States has PFAS in their blood.
contentParagraphs.zh: PFAS are strong and can repel both water and oil, which is why they're widely used in manufacturing. But they don't break down easily in the environment, and they can linger in our bodies. In fact, according to the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, nearly everyone in the United States has PFAS in their blood.

contentParagraphs.en: "They have really unique properties, and that's part of what has made them so attractive to industry. Unfortunately, we're learning that it is also something that makes them toxic and causes problems in the human body," says Megan Romano, an epidemiologist at Dartmouth who studies PFAS.
contentParagraphs.zh: "They have really unique properties, and that's part of what has made them so attractive to industry. Unfortunately, we're learning that it is also something that makes them toxic and causes problems in the human body," says Megan Romano, an epidemiologist at Dartmouth who studies PFAS.

contentParagraphs.en: Eliminating all sources of PFAS in your life would be impossible, according to one report from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine. And researchers are still trying to pin down which actions reduce exposure.
contentParagraphs.zh: Eliminating all sources of PFAS in your life would be impossible, according to one report from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine. And researchers are still trying to pin down which actions reduce exposure.

contentParagraphs.en: But it's worth trying. Research has found that when people remove their exposures, the levels of these contaminants in their bodies decrease over the course of several years.
contentParagraphs.zh: But it's worth trying. Research has found that when people remove their exposures, the levels of these contaminants in their bodies decrease over the course of several years.

contentParagraphs.en: If you'd like to reduce your exposure to PFAS, there are a few actions you can take. Some are simple, like looking for third-party-tested PFAS-free products. Others aren't as straightforward; the U.S. is still trying to figure out where these chemicals are showing up in food and water.
contentParagraphs.zh: If you'd like to reduce your exposure to PFAS, there are a few actions you can take. Some are simple, like looking for third-party-tested PFAS-free products. Others aren't as straightforward; the U.S. is still trying to figure out where these chemicals are showing up in food and water.

contentParagraphs.en: Check if your water has been tested
contentParagraphs.zh: Check if your water has been tested

contentParagraphs.en: If your community has water contaminated by PFAS chemicals, drinking water could be your main source of exposure. According to the U.S. Geological Survey, they're in nearly half of the nation's tap water.
contentParagraphs.zh: If your community has water contaminated by PFAS chemicals, drinking water could be your main source of exposure. According to the U.S. Geological Survey, they're in nearly half of the nation's tap water.

contentParagraphs.en: Many cities and towns have already tested public water for these chemicals, so a good first step is to check with your water utility to see if they have published those results. To do that, you can call your utility's customer service line or look online to see if they've published PFAS data in water quality reports.
contentParagraphs.zh: Many cities and towns have already tested public water for these chemicals, so a good first step is to check with your water utility to see if they have published those results. To do that, you can call your utility's customer service line or look online to see if they've published PFAS data in water quality reports.

contentParagraphs.en: In many states, environmental regulators may also be able to share information about PFAS levels in public water. The deadline for water utilities to test for PFAS chemicals under the current Environmental Protection Agency regulations is 2027, so if results aren't available yet, they should be by then.
contentParagraphs.zh: In many states, environmental regulators may also be able to share information about PFAS levels in public water. The deadline for water utilities to test for PFAS chemicals under the current Environmental Protection Agency regulations is 2027, so if results aren't available yet, they should be by then.

contentParagraphs.en: If you're on a private well instead of on public water, you may need to test your water yourself. Some states have programs to help with the expense and logistics.
contentParagraphs.zh: If you're on a private well instead of on public water, you may need to test your water yourself. Some states have programs to help with the expense and logistics.

contentParagraphs.en: The U.S. Geological Survey has a map that shows their testing results across the country. An interactive EPA map shows testing results for public water systems, with systems testing above national limits in brown. Another map, from a nonprofit called the Environmental Working Group, shows results from several national testing efforts.
contentParagraphs.zh: The U.S. Geological Survey has a map that shows their testing results across the country. An interactive EPA map shows testing results for public water systems, with systems testing above national limits in brown. Another map, from a nonprofit called the Environmental Working Group, shows results from several national testing efforts.

contentParagraphs.en: Once you figure out the levels of PFAS chemicals in your water, you can compare them to the EPA's regulations. You can also enter them into the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's blood level estimation tool, which can give you a sense of what the levels of these chemicals in your blood might be.
contentParagraphs.zh: Once you figure out the levels of PFAS chemicals in your water, you can compare them to the EPA's regulations. You can also enter them into the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's blood level estimation tool, which can give you a sense of what the levels of these chemicals in your blood might be.

contentParagraphs.en: See if your community is installing filters, or consider getting your own
contentParagraphs.zh: See if your community is installing filters, or consider getting your own

contentParagraphs.en: If you find out your water has levels of PFAS chemicals above the EPA's limits, check to see if your community is planning to install a filtration system that can catch those substances. Public water systems are required to reduce PFAS in water by 2029, or by 2031 under an extension proposed by the Trump administration.
contentParagraphs.zh: If you find out your water has levels of PFAS chemicals above the EPA's limits, check to see if your community is planning to install a filtration system that can catch those substances. Public water systems are required to reduce PFAS in water by 2029, or by 2031 under an extension proposed by the Trump administration.

contentParagraphs.en: There are a few kinds of treatment systems that work on PFAS contamination: activated carbon filters, ion exchange treatment and reverse osmosis.
contentParagraphs.zh: There are a few kinds of treatment systems that work on PFAS contamination: activated carbon filters, ion exchange treatment and reverse osmosis.

contentParagraphs.en: If you decide to get your own filter, make sure it is certified to treat PFAS contamination. You'll want to look for certifications from NSF International and the American National Standards Institute (ANSI). Specifically, filters that are "NSF/ANSI 53"- or "NSF/ANSI 58"-certified should work to reduce PFAS chemicals — though their current requirements aren't based on the latest EPA limits.
contentParagraphs.zh: If you decide to get your own filter, make sure it is certified to treat PFAS contamination. You'll want to look for certifications from NSF International and the American National Standards Institute (ANSI). Specifically, filters that are "NSF/ANSI 53"- or "NSF/ANSI 58"-certified should work to reduce PFAS chemicals — though their current requirements aren't based on the latest EPA limits.

contentParagraphs.en: The upfront cost of in-home water treatment can range from around $20 for pitcher-style filters to hundreds or thousands of dollars for whole-home systems. But make sure to factor in maintenance costs, too — it's important to follow the instructions that come with the treatment system and replace parts as needed.

But make sure to factor in maintenance costs, too — it's important to follow the instructions that come with the treatment system and replace parts as needed.

contentParagraphs.en: Consider your food
contentParagraphs.zh: Consider your food

contentParagraphs.en: When PFAS chemicals get into the environment, they can make their way into our food.
contentParagraphs.zh: When PFAS chemicals get into the environment, they can make their way into our food.

contentParagraphs.en: According to the National Academies report, diet is likely the primary way people are exposed to PFAS in places without drinking water contamination. But, researchers say, longer-term studies are needed to assess how effective different interventions might be for reducing exposure from food.
contentParagraphs.zh: According to the National Academies report, diet is likely the primary way people are exposed to PFAS in places without drinking water contamination. But, researchers say, longer-term studies are needed to assess how effective different interventions might be for reducing exposure from food.

contentParagraphs.en: Some meat, dairy products and chicken eggs have been found to contain PFAS chemicals, but initial results from Food and Drug Administration research show that most detectable PFAS were found in seafood. Federal regulators recommend people avoid eating fish from waterways that have been impacted by PFAS. Produce grown with contaminated water can also become a source of exposure.

articles.title: Takeaways from Iowa's primaries. And, DOJ nixes Trump's 'anti-weaponization' fund
articles.summary: Polls have now closed in six states that held primary elections yesterday. Here are the key takeaways. And, the Justice Department has scrapped plans for Trump's "anti-weaponization" fund.
articles.titleZh: 爱荷华州初选的要点。而且，司法部取消了特朗普的“反武器化”基金
articles.summaryZh: 昨天举行初选的六个州的投票现已结束。以下是要点。而且，司法部已经取消了特朗普的“反武器化”基金计划。

contentParagraphs.en: Good morning. You're reading the Up First newsletter. Subscribe here to get it delivered to your inbox, and listen to the Up First podcast for all the news you need to start your day.
contentParagraphs.zh: 早上好。您正在阅读 Up First 时事通讯。在此订阅，将其发送到您的收件箱，并收听 Up First 播客，了解开始新的一天所需的所有新闻。

contentParagraphs.en: Today's top stories
contentParagraphs.zh: 今日头条新闻

contentParagraphs.en: Trump-endorsed Congressman Randy Feenstra lost the Iowa gubernatorial primary race last night to businessman Zach Lahn. It's a rare blow to President Trump, who has seen almost all of his chosen candidates succeed. Farmers in Iowa have been disproportionately impacted by Trump administration policies like tariffs and the war in Iran. The state is governed by Republicans, but voters have cast their ballots for Democrats in recent federal and state elections. Democratic voters are strategically selecting candidates they believe will best position them to flip seats in November.
contentParagraphs.zh: 特朗普支持的国会议员兰迪·芬斯特拉昨晚在爱荷华州州长初选中输给了商人扎克·兰。这对特朗普总统来说是一个罕见的打击，他几乎所有选定的候选人都取得了成功。爱荷华州的农民受到关税和伊朗战争等特朗普政府政策的影响尤为严重。该州由共和党执政，但选民在最近的联邦和州选举中将选票投给了民主党。民主党选民正在战略性地选择他们认为最有利于他们在 11 月翻转席位的候选人。

contentParagraphs.en: Voters fill out ballots at Lowe Park Arts and Environment Center on Tuesday in Marion, Iowa. Iowans voting in today's primary election will nominate candidates for governor, U.S. Senate, and U.S. House in advance of this fall's midterm elections.
contentParagraphs.zh: 选民周二在爱荷华州马里昂的洛公园艺术与环境中心填写选票。 Iowans voting in today's primary election will nominate candidates for governor, U.S. Senate, and U.S. House in advance of this fall's midterm elections.

contentParagraphs.en: 🎧 In the upcoming midterm elections, Lahn will compete against Rob Sand, the Democratic nominee who ran unopposed in the primaries. Sand is also the only statewide Democratic elected official. The race between the two politicians begins as a true toss-up, NPR's Stephen Fowler tells Up First.➡️ Here are the results so far for the six states that held primaries yesterday.➡️ The California governor's primary election remains too close to call. Democrat Xavier Becerra and Republican Steve Hilton lead the field, with Democrat Tom Steyer in third.
contentParagraphs.zh: 🎧 在即将到来的中期选举中，拉恩将与在初选中无人反对的民主党候选人罗布·桑德竞争。桑德也是全州唯一的民主党民选官员。 NPR 的史蒂芬·福勒 (Stephen Fowler) 告诉 Up First，两位政客之间的竞争一开始就是一场真正的势均力敌。➡️ 以下是昨天举行初选的六个州迄今为止的结果。➡️ 加州州长初选仍然势均力敌。民主党人泽维尔·贝塞拉和共和党人史蒂夫·希尔顿领先，民主党人汤姆·斯泰尔位居第三。

contentParagraphs.en: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche told members of Congress yesterday that plans for Trump's $1.776 billion anti-weaponization fund are no longer moving forward. His remarks come just days after a federal judge temporarily blocked the creation of the fund after Democracy Forward and other organizations filed a lawsuit.
contentParagraphs.zh: 代理司法部长托德·布兰奇昨天告诉国会议员，特朗普 17.76 亿美元反武器化基金的计划不再向前推进。就在他发表上述言论的几天前，一名联邦法官在民主前进和其他组织提起诉讼后暂时阻止了该基金的设立。

contentParagraphs.en: 🎧 In a rare occurrence, the fund has prompted many Republicans, especially in the Senate, to publicly oppose Trump, NPR's Ryan Lucas says. Many Republicans disliked the possibility that payouts could go to Capitol rioters who attacked police on Jan. 6, 2021. This Republican pushback has stalled some of the administration's legislative priorities, including funding for immigration enforcement. From the beginning, Democrats have criticized the fund, arguing that it would serve as a slush fund that allows the president to give nearly $1.8 billion in taxpayer money to his supporters and allies.
contentParagraphs.zh: NPR 的瑞安·卢卡斯 (Ryan Lucas) 表示，该基金促使许多共和党人，尤其是参议院的共和党人公开反对特朗普，这是罕见的情况。许多共和党人不喜欢向 2021 年 1 月 6 日袭击警察的国会大厦骚乱者支付赔偿金。共和党的这种抵制已经阻碍了政府的一些立法优先事项，包括为移民执法提供资金。从一开始，民主党人就批评该基金，认为它将充当行贿基金，让总统可以向他的支持者和盟友提供近 18 亿美元的纳税人资金。

contentParagraphs.en: Trump announced yesterday that he is appointing Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte as the acting director of national intelligence. Pulte will replace Tulsi Gabbard, who is stepping down at the end of the month. His biography on the FHFA website highlights his career experience in housing and philanthropy, but it does not mention any background in intelligence.
contentParagraphs.zh: 特朗普昨天宣布，他将任命联邦住房金融局局长比尔·普尔特担任代理国家情报总监。普尔特将接替将于本月底辞职的图尔西·加巴德。 FHFA 网站上的他的传记强调了他在住房和慈善事业方面的职业经历，但没有提及任何情报背景。

contentParagraphs.en: 🎧 The law that established the director of national intelligence position after 9/11 says that all nominees must have "extensive national security experience," NPR's Danielle Kurtzleben says. But Pulte isn't a nominee. Trump has named him as the acting director, meaning he won't go through a Senate confirmation. Kurtzleben says this role is a key position as the U.S. engages with foreign threats worldwide, especially during the war with Iran. Pulte has used his position as head of the Federal Housing Finance Agency to legally pursue Trump's perceived enemies. He has accused multiple people of mortgage fraud, including New York Attorney General Letitia James and Democratic Sen. Adam Schiff.
contentParagraphs.zh: NPR 的丹妮尔·库茨莱本 (Danielle Kurtzleben) 表示，9/11 事件后设立国家情报总监职位的法律规定，所有提名人都必须拥有“丰富的国家安全经验”。但普尔特不是提名人。特朗普已任命他为代理主任，这意味着他不会经过参议院的确认。库尔茨莱本表示，在美国应对全球范围内的外国威胁时，特别是在与伊朗的战争期间，这一角色是一个关键角色。普尔特利用联邦住房金融局局长的职位，合法追捕特朗普的敌人。他指控多人进行抵押贷款欺诈，其中包括纽约总检察长利蒂西亚·詹姆斯和民主党参议员亚当·希夫。

contentParagraphs.en: Researchers are speaking out against a new White House web page, saying that it uses dehumanizing language to target immigrants. The site aliens.gov discusses the threat of "aliens," accompanied by the opening theme of The X-Files. The webpage equates undocumented immigrants with extraterrestrials. The administration refers to these individuals as an "it" and claims "they do not belong here." While some may dismiss the wordplay as a joke, Ernesto Castañeda, director of the Center for Latin American and Latino Studies at American University, says that "for a few people, it may be another license to act violently against people that they may think are aliens, undocumented. They may be another incitement to hate crimes and to profiling people."
contentParagraphs.zh: 研究人员公开反对白宫的一个新网页，称该网页使用非人性语言来针对移民。 Aliens.gov 网站以《X 档案》的开场主题讨论了“外星人”的威胁。该网页将无证移民等同于外星人。政府将这些人称为“它”，并声称“他们不属于这里”。虽然有些人可能认为这个双关语是个笑话，但美利坚大学拉丁美洲和拉丁裔研究中心主任埃内斯托·卡斯塔涅达表示，“对于一些人来说，这可能是对他们认为是外国人、无证移民的暴力行为的又一许可。这可能是又一煽动仇恨犯罪和对他人进行分析的行为。”

articles.title: UK media websites given power to block Google using their articles in AI search
articles.summary:
Watchdog makes ruling on search summaries after publishers complain about drop in click-through traffic and revenue

Business live – latest updates

Online publishers and news organisations are now able to block their content from appearing in Google’s AI summaries in UK search r
articles.titleZh: 英国媒体网站有权阻止谷歌在人工智能搜索中使用其文章
articles.summaryZh:
在出版商抱怨点击流量和收入下降后，监管机构对搜索摘要做出裁决

商业直播 – 最新更新

在线出版商和新闻机构现在可以阻止其内容出现在英国搜索引擎中的 Google 人工智能摘要中

contentParagraphs.en: Online publishers and news organisations are now able to block their content from appearing in Google’s AI summaries in UK search results, the British competition watchdog has announced.The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said the new requirement would “put publishers, like news organisations, in a stronger position to negotiate content deals with Google”.Many media organisations have complained that they have seen a drop in click-through traffic to their websites – and therefore their revenue – since Google started placing AI summaries at the top of its search page. Many people read these without clicking through to the original journalism.Up until now, websites were also unable to opt out of their content being scraped for AI overviews without also withdrawing from traditional Google search. Given the company’s market dominance, this would have reduced the visibility of their journalism.The News Media Association, which represents UK news publishers including the Guardian, said the decision was a “significant step towards levelling the playing field and building a fair, transparent digital economy where premium content is properly respected and fairly compensated”.Google said it would start testing a new control from Wednesday on a subset of UK-based media sites, allowing owners to manage how their links and content appear in its AI search features, with the aim of rolling out the controls globally.The new measures are among those announced by the CMA to loosen Google’s grip on search, which the watchdog said would “secure a fairer deal for publishers and consumers and improve Google’s search services in the UK”.It said Google would now be required to make sure that publisher content was properly attributed, using clear links, in AI-generated search results, in order to boost consumer trust.The CMA said the new measures fell under the UK’s digital markets competition regime, and followed on from its decision to designate Google with strategic market status in general search services. It first proposed the idea of an AI search summary opt-out in January.Sarah Cardell, the watchdog’s chief executive, said: “It is crucial that content publishers, including news organisations, have appropriate bargaining power over how their content is used.”She called this a “world‑first requirement on Google’s search services in the UK” and said it would enable “fair treatment, greater transparency and meaningful choice for businesses and consumers”.Google accounts for more than 90% of general searches in the UK, according to the CMA, and as a result publishers and news organisations have relied on search results to drive consumers to their websites.The CMA has previously estimated that search advertising costs the equivalent of nearly £500 for each UK household a year, which could be kept down with effective competition.It said Google had announced significant changes to its search platform in May, which could fundamentally change how search results are presented to UK users. The new conduct requirement would also apply to these changes, the CMA said, adding that it would monitor Google’s conduct and assess the implications for businesses.Tom Lewis, a competition lawyer at Geradin Partners and a former CMA director who represent news publishers on Google search issues, said the changes would help news publishers keep some control over how the US company uses their work.“The CMA should be congratulated for tackling this issue. They are aiming to open up competition on the key digital platforms,” he said, adding that Google’s plan to roll out changes globally was a “big win”.The watchdog said it would be making further announcements related to Google’s search business in the coming weeks.Google said in a blogpost published on Wednesday that it was engaging with regulators such as the CMA “to ensure website owners have the right tools as user preferences evolve”.Mrinalini Loew, the general manager at Google Search Ecosystem, said it was beginning to test a tool allowing website owners to manage how their links and content appear in its AI search features, such as AI overviews and AI mode.“We are beginning to roll these features out to a subset of website owners in the UK, allowing for thorough testing before rolling them out to website owners globally,” she said.The control will not be used as a ranking signal for search results outside the generative AI search features, Loew added.

articles.title: Post Office scandal victim dedicates OBE to 'sub-postmasters we have lost'
articles.summary: The 92-year-old said she dedicate the OBE to 'all sub-postmasters we have lost'.
articles.titleZh: 邮局丑闻受害者将官佐勋章献给“我们失去的副邮政局长”
articles.summaryZh: 这位 92 岁的老人说，她将 OBE 勋章献给“我们失去的所有副邮政局长”。

contentParagraphs.en: Betty Brown, the oldest surviving victim of the Post Office Horizon IT scandal, will receive an OBE at Windsor Castle today. Brown was one of hundreds of sub-postmasters wrongly accused of stealing, and was forced out of her County Durham branch in 2003 - despite her and her late husband spending more than £50,000 of their savings to make good on losses which didn't exist. The award "won't be a Betty Brown medal," the former sub-postmaster said, but will be for "all the sub postmasters that we have lost". The Horizon IT system was responsible for more than 900 sub-postmasters being wrongfully prosecuted because of it providing incorrect information. Thousands were forced to make up for the alleged losses at their branches across the UK. More than a billion pounds has been paid out in compensation to victims of the scandal, according to the government.

articles.title: Iran, Kuwait, Bahrain hit: Is the war in the Gulf escalating again?
articles.summary: Secretary of State Rubio tells US lawmakers the war on Iran is over, amid recent attacks on Iran and Gulf countries.
articles.titleZh: 伊朗、科威特、巴林遭遇重创：海湾战事再度升级？
articles.summaryZh: 在最近针对伊朗和海湾国家的袭击之后，国务卿卢比奥告诉美国国会议员，对伊朗的战争已经结束。

contentParagraphs.en: Iran fired missiles at Kuwait and Bahrain in the early hours of Wednesday, and the United States launched strikes on Iran’s Qeshm Island, as tensions in the Gulf spiked amid an impasse in diplomatic efforts to end the war between Tehran and Washington that is now closing in on 100 days.Since the conflict began in late February, Iran has repeatedly targeted countries across the Gulf where US military bases are located. Armed hostilities had largely subsided after a temporary Pakistan-mediated ceasefire took effect on April 8. But the latest flare‑up has raised fears that full‑scale fighting could resume.Here is a look at what happened overnight, and where things stand on the diplomatic front.What happened in Kuwait and Bahrain?Kuwait’s state news agency KUNA said Iranian missiles and drones had hit the country’s international airport on Wednesday morning. It reported an unspecified number of injuries, damage to airport facilities and flight suspensions and diversions.India’s foreign ministry condemned the attack on the Kuwait International Airport. In a statement released on Wednesday, the ministry said that an Indian national has been killed and “several of our nationals are injured”.“We offer our deepest condolences to the bereaved family. Our Embassy is extending all possible assistance to those injured. We remain in close contact with local authorities for the welfare of Indian nationals,” it said.But US Central Command (CENTCOM) said two Iranian missiles shot at Kuwait fell short or broke apart in flight, while several ballistic missiles failed to reach their targets. Iran’s semi-official Tasnim news agency earlier said the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) had launched missiles and drones at US helicopters stationed in a regional country, in an apparent reference to Kuwait.It is unclear whether the US helicopters the IRGC was reportedly targeting were stationed at the Kuwaiti airport or whether intercepted projectiles fell by accident on the airport.According to Tasnim, the IRGC also fired missiles and drones at an airbase and the headquarters of the US Fifth Fleet in Bahrain, where sirens went off warning residents of an attack.However, CENTCOM said it had intercepted the projectiles aimed at Bahrain. It also said no US personnel or assets were harmed in the attacks on Kuwait and Bahrain.Was Iran also hit?Just before Iran fired missiles and drones at Kuwait and Bahrain, US forces hit a telecommunications tower on Iran’s Qeshm Island, which sits in the Gulf and is believed to be a repository of the country’s missiles stored in underground facilities.CENTCOM said the US military also downed Iranian drones targeting civilian ships in regional waters.Tehran said US forces struck an Iranian oil tanker near the Strait of Hormuz, damaging its engine room. Iranian media reported the IRGC’s navy targeted a vessel it identified as “Panaya” with missiles in response to the attack on the Iranian tanker.Hold on, so who hit whom first?The US and Iran have competing narratives. But the Trump administration has made clear that it will not allow Iranian oil to pass through the Strait of Hormuz as it maintains its blockade of Iranian ports and ships. The escalation on Wednesday morning appears to have begun with the US hit on the Iranian oil tanker. Both sides appear to concur that Iran then attempted to strike other vessels in the Gulf. The US says it shot down the Iranian drones fired at ships and then hit Qeshm Island. Iran hit back by firing at Kuwait and Bahrain. What has Iran said about Wednesday’s developments?The Ministry of Foreign Affairs condemned the US attacks on Qeshm Island as a violation of the ceasefire.It said Kuwait and Bahrain bore “direct and clear responsibility” for the attacks, alleging their territory and facilities had been used to support US military operations against Iran.Iran said it reserved the right to self-defence and would use all available means to respond, including by targeting the source of any future attacks.“Disrupting the security of the Strait of Hormuz will carry a heavy price for the US military,” Iranian media reported, citing the IRGC.What is the state of diplomacy?Secretary of State Marco Rubio told lawmakers on Tuesday that the US would agree to sanctions relief only if Iran agrees to give up its nuclear activity.“The war is over,” Rubio declared during a sharp exchange with Democratic Senator Cory Booker of New Jersey, who disagreed.He also told Congress that Iran’s supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, is alive and becoming “increasingly engaged” in negotiations with Washington. Khamenei has not appeared publicly since reportedly being wounded in US-Israeli strikes that killed his predecessor and father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.Tehran is seeking access to billions of dollars in oil revenues, waivers on crude exports, a lifting of a US blockade on its ports and continued leverage over the strait, which handled a fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas traffic before the war.Iran’s chief negotiator, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, said during a conversation with Lebanese Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri that Tehran could abandon negotiations with the US and move towards confrontation if Israeli attacks on Lebanon continue.How have attacks escalated recently?After weeks of a lull following the ceasefire, the US and Iran have increased their exchange of attacks in recent weeks.Late on Sunday, CENTCOM said it conducted “self-defense strikes” on Iranian radar and drone sites in the city of Goruk and on Qeshm Island over the weekend.A day later, the general staff of the Kuwaiti armed forces said its air defences were “confronting hostile missile and drone attacks”. If sounds of explosions are heard, they are the result of air defences intercepting the projectiles, it added.On May 17, authorities in Abu Dhabi, UAE, said a single drone strike caused a blaze to break out at an electrical generator outside the Barakah plant’s inner perimeter in the al-Dha
contentParagraphs.zh: 周三凌晨，伊朗向科威特和巴林发射了导弹，美国对伊朗格什姆岛发动了袭击。在结束德黑兰和华盛顿之间战争的外交努力陷入僵局之际，海湾地区紧张局势加剧，这场战争现已接近100天。自2月下旬冲突开始以来，伊朗多次以美国军事基地所在的海湾国家为目标。 4月8日，巴基斯坦斡旋的临时停火生效后，武装敌对行动已基本平息。但最新的冲突引发了人们对全面战斗可能恢复的担忧。以下是昨晚发生的事情以及外交方面的情况。科威特和巴林发生了什么？科威特国家通讯社KUNA称，伊朗导弹和无人机周三早上袭击了该国国际机场。据报道，伤亡人数不详，机场设施受损，航班暂停和改道。印度外交部谴责科威特国际机场的袭击事件。在周三发布的一份声明中，国防部表示，一名印度国民被杀，“我们的几名国民受伤”。声明称，“我们向死者家属表示最深切的哀悼。我们的大使馆正在向受伤者提供一切可能的援助。为了印度国民的福祉，我们与地方当局保持密切联系。”但美国中央司令部表示，向科威特发射的两枚伊朗导弹没有射中或在飞行中破裂，而几枚弹道导弹未能到达目标。伊朗半官方通讯社塔斯尼姆通讯社早些时候称，伊斯兰革命卫队向驻扎在该地区国家的美国直升机发射了导弹和无人机，显然是指科威特。目前尚不清楚伊朗革命卫队所针对的美国直升机是否驻扎在科威特机场，或者拦截的炮弹是否意外落在机场。据塔斯尼姆通讯社报道，伊朗革命卫队还向空军基地和总部发射了导弹和无人机。驻巴林的美国第五舰队的警报响起，警告居民遭受袭击。不过，中央司令部表示，它已经拦截了瞄准巴林的炮弹。它还表示，在对科威特和巴林的袭击中，没有美国人员或资产受到伤害。伊朗也受到打击吗？就在伊朗向科威特和巴林发射导弹和无人机之前，美军击中了伊朗格什姆岛上的一座电信塔，该岛位于海湾，据信是该国储存在地下设施中的导弹的储存库。中央司令部称，美军还击落了针对地区水域民用船只的伊朗无人机。德黑兰称，美军击落了一艘伊朗油轮霍尔木兹海峡附近，损坏了其机舱。伊朗媒体报道称，伊朗革命卫队海军用导弹瞄准了一艘被称为“Panaya”的船只，以回应伊朗油轮遭到的袭击。等等，那么谁先攻击了谁呢？美国和伊朗有着相互矛盾的说法。但特朗普政府已明确表示，不会允许伊朗石油通过霍尔木兹海峡，同时仍维持对伊朗港口和船只的封锁。周三上午的升级似乎是从美国袭击伊朗油轮开始的。双方似乎都同意伊朗随后试图袭击海湾的其他船只。美国表示，它击落了向船只发射的伊朗无人机，然后袭击了格什姆岛。伊朗向科威特和巴林开火进行反击。伊朗对周三的事态发展有何评论？外交部谴责美国对格什姆岛的袭击违反了停火协议。伊朗表示，科威特和巴林对这些袭击负有“直接和明确的责任”，称其领土和设施被用来支持美国对伊朗的军事行动。伊朗表示保留自卫权利，并将使用一切可用手段进行回应，包括针对未来任何袭击的来源。“破坏霍尔木兹海峡的安全将带来伊朗媒体援引伊朗伊斯兰革命卫队的话说，“这对美国军队来说是沉重的代价”。外交状况如何？国务卿马可·卢比奥周二对议员们说，只有伊朗同意放弃核活动，美国才会同意解除制裁。卢比奥在与新泽西州民主党参议员科里·布克的激烈交锋中宣称，“战争已经结束”，但后者不同意。他还告诉国会，伊朗最高领导人莫杰塔巴·哈梅内伊还活着，并且正在成为一名伊朗领导人。 “越来越多地参与”与华盛顿的谈判。据报道，哈梅内伊在美以袭击中受伤，其前任兼父亲阿亚图拉·阿里·哈梅内伊 (Ayatollah Ali Khamenei) 被打死，此后哈梅内伊就没有公开露面。德黑兰正在寻求获得数十亿美元的石油收入、原油出口豁免、解除美国对其港口的封锁以及继续控制该海峡，该海峡在战前处理了世界五分之一的石油和液化天然气运输。伊朗首席谈判代表穆罕默德·巴格尔 (Mohammad Bagher)加利巴夫在与黎巴嫩议会议长纳比·贝里的谈话中表示，如果以色列继续袭击黎巴嫩，德黑兰可能会放弃与美国的谈判，转向对抗。最近袭击如何升级？停火后几周的平静之后，美国和伊朗最近几周增加了相互攻击。周日晚些时候，中央司令部表示，它对戈鲁克市和格什姆岛的伊朗雷达和无人机站点进行了“自卫袭击”一天后，科威特武装部队总参谋部表示，其防空系统正在“应对敌方导弹和无人机袭击”。声明补充说，如果听到爆炸声，则是防空系统拦截射弹造成的。 5 月 17 日，阿联酋阿布扎比当局表示，一次无人机袭击导致阿尔达哈省巴拉卡工厂内围外的发电机起火。
